era_context: The early 8th century CE spans the height of the Umayyad Caliphate in the Middle East and North Africa, the Tang Dynasty's consolidation of power in China, the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms in Britain navigating internal and external pressures, and the emergence of Visigothic instability in Iberia. This is a period of rapid Islamic expansion, sophisticated court cultures in both the Islamic world and China, and significant religious and political transformation across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written sources; archaeological record incomplete)
Northern Europe beyond Anglo-Saxon kingdoms (sparse contemporary documentation)
Oceania and Pacific regions (oral traditions not yet systematically recorded in surviving texts)

events.title: Death of Caliph al-Walid I and succession of Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik
events.region: Umayyad Caliphate (Syria, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, Iberia)
events.description: Al-Walid I, one of the most successful Umayyad caliphs, died in February 715 CE after a reign marked by military expansion and architectural patronage (including the Umayyad Mosque in Damascus). His successor, Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik, took the throne and continued expansionist policies, particularly in North Africa and the Mediterranean. Sulayman's reign would be characterized by continued military campaigns and administrative consolidation, though his health declined significantly toward the end of his caliphate.
key_figures: Sulayman ibn Abd al-Malik (Umayyad Caliph, r. 715–717)

sources.name: Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings (Tarikh al-Rusul wa-al-Muluk)
Ibn al-Athir, Complete History (al-Kamil fi al-Tarikh)

events.certainty_note: Death date of al-Walid I is well-attested in Islamic historiography, with consistent dating across multiple sources compiled within 200–300 years of the event.

events.title: Berber and Arab forces under Tariq ibn Ziyad prepare for invasion of Visigothic Iberia
events.description: In the context of Umayyad expansion and internal Visigothic civil conflict, preparations intensified for the crossing into Iberia. While the major invasion is traditionally dated to 711 CE, military and diplomatic groundwork was laid in 715 and preceding years. Berber and Arab forces under the command of Tariq ibn Ziyad, a Berber general serving the Umayyad governor Musa ibn Nusayr, assembled in North Africa. The crossing would be facilitated by internal Visigothic factionalism and the invitation of one faction seeking external military support.
key_figures: Musa ibn Nusayr (Umayyad governor of North Africa)

sources.name: Ibn Abd al-Hakam, History of the Conquest of Egypt and the Maghreb (Futuh Misr wa-al-Maghreb)
Al-Tabari, History of the Prophets and Kings

events.certainty_note: The invasion itself is dated to 711 CE in most sources; 715 represents the preparatory phase, inferred from the timeline of military mobilization and political circumstances rather than explicit contemporary documentation.

events.title: Tang Dynasty consolidation under Emperor Xuanzong's early reign
events.description: Emperor Xuanzong of Tang ascended to the throne in 712 CE and by 715 CE was consolidating power after the tumultuous reign of his father Ruizong. Xuanzong's early years saw the removal of the powerful Wu Zetian faction and the stabilization of court politics. He appointed capable administrators and military commanders, laying the groundwork for what would become the most prosperous and culturally vibrant period of the Tang Dynasty. This period saw renewed attention to Confucian governance and the examination system.

sources.name: Jiu Tangshu (Old History of Tang), compiled by Liu Xu et al.
Xin Tangshu (New History of Tang), compiled by Ouyang Xiu and Song Qi

events.certainty_note: Xuanzong's accession and early consolidation are well-documented in Tang official histories compiled within 200–300 years of the events.

events.title: Anglo-Saxon ecclesiastical and political developments in Britain
events.description: In early 8th-century Britain, the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms were experiencing significant ecclesiastical development following the Synod of Whitby (664 CE) and the consolidation of Roman Christian practices. By 715 CE, monasteries and episcopal sees were well-established across Northumbria, Mercia, East Anglia, and Wessex. Monastic scriptoria were producing illuminated manuscripts and theological works. The period saw the flourishing of Northumbrian scholarship and the beginning of what would become the Lindisfarne Gospel tradition (though the famous Lindisfarne Gospels are dated to c. 700 CE).
key_figures: Bede (Venerable Bede, c. 673–735, Northumbrian monk and historian)

sources.name: Bede, Ecclesiastical History of the English People (Historia ecclesiastica gentis Anglorum)

events.certainty_note: General ecclesiastical developments are well-attested; specific events for 715 CE are not individually documented in surviving sources, but the broader context is confirmed.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional dating of the Visigothic invasion of Iberia to 711 CE has been questioned by some scholars who argue for a slightly earlier or later date (710–713 CE range), though 711 CE remains the scholarly consensus. The extent of Tariq ibn Ziyad's role versus Musa ibn Nusayr's role in the invasion is debated in modern scholarship, with some sources emphasizing Musa's primacy. No disconfirming evidence identified for other events listed.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 715 CE is uneven. Islamic sources are relatively rich but compiled 200+ years after the events, introducing potential distortion through oral transmission and later theological interpolation. Anglo-Saxon and Tang sources are contemporary or near-contemporary but focus on elite political and ecclesiastical matters; the experiences of common people are largely absent. Sub-Saharan African, Slavic, and Mesoamerican developments are virtually absent from surviving written records for this year, reflecting the geographic bias of surviving documentation toward the Mediterranean, Middle East, and East Asia.

graph_edges.note: Sulayman's succession continued the expansionist policies that culminated in the Iberian invasion
Xuanzong's consolidation of power in 715 CE set the stage for the prosperous mid-Tang period until the An Lushan Rebellion in 755–756 CE
